title: Ezekiel 18
subtitle: The Soul Who Sins Shall Die

sections.header: Verses 1-18 — Three Generations: Righteous, Wicked, Righteous

heb.gloss: the soul who sins — it shall die
heb.paragraph: This declaration is the theological thesis of the chapter and one of the most important statements in the OT. Each individual is accountable for their own sin. The righteous father does not guarantee a righteous son; the wicked father does not condemn a righteous son. Moral identity is personal, not hereditary.

panels.ctx: The exiles quoted a proverb: 'The fathers eat sour grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge' — meaning they blamed their suffering on their ancestors' sins. God abolishes this proverb with a three-generation case study: a righteous man (lives), his wicked son (dies), and his righteous grandson (lives). Each generation stands or falls on its own moral conduct. This is Ezekiel's definitive statement on individual responsibility.

cross.note: Jeremiah also quotes and refutes the same proverb — both prophets independently combat the theology of inherited guilt.
Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor children for their fathers — the legal precedent for individual accountability.
God will render to each according to their works — Paul restates the principle of individual judgment.

notes.note: God abolishes the proverb: 'As I live, you will no longer use this proverb in Israel.' The theology of inherited guilt is formally rejected.
The righteous man's profile: does not eat at mountain shrines, does not oppress, does not commit adultery, gives bread to the hungry. Righteousness is defined concretely, not abstractly.
The wicked son does the opposite: violence, oppression, idolatry. A righteous father cannot save a wicked son from the consequences of his own choices.
The righteous grandson who sees his father's sins and refuses to repeat them will live. Breaking the cycle of sin is possible through individual moral choice.
Calvin uses this passage to teach that God's justice is never arbitrary. Each person is judged according to their own conduct. The complaint of inherited guilt is a denial of divine justice.

The list of righteous behaviors mixes cultic and ethical requirements: not eating at mountain shrines (cultic) alongside not oppressing anyone (ethical). For Ezekiel the priest, worship and justice are inseparable.
The proverb about sour grapes was apparently widespread among the exiles — both Jeremiah and Ezekiel address it independently, suggesting a pervasive theology of victimhood.

Block sees this chapter as the theological foundation laid by the watchman pericope (3:16-21). The watchman warned individuals; now God explains why individual warning matters — because individuals, not families or nations, stand before God. Corporate identity does not erase personal accountability.

Zimmerli identifies the three-generation schema as a casuistic legal form (case law): if a man does X, he lives; if his son does Y, he dies. The priestly-legal structure transforms theology into jurisprudence, reflecting Ezekiel's priestly training.

heb.gloss: to turn / to repent / to return
heb.paragraph: The verb shuv (turn/return) is the OT's primary word for repentance. It means a complete reversal of direction — not mere regret but a changed life. God pleads: 'Turn! Turn from your evil ways! Why will you die, house of Israel?' The double imperative reveals divine urgency — God does not desire the death of anyone.

panels.ctx: The chapter climaxes with one of the most important theological declarations in the OT: God takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked but desires that they turn and live (v.23, restated in 33:11). This transforms the portrait of God from a judge eager to punish to a father pleading for his children to choose life. The invitation to repent is not an afterthought but the point of the entire oracle.

cross.note: As I live, I take no pleasure in the death of the wicked — the same declaration restated after Jerusalem's fall.
The Lord is not slow about his promise but is patient, not wishing any to perish but all to come to repentance — Peter echoes Ezekiel's theology.
There is more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents — Jesus embodies the God who desires repentance.

notes.note: If a wicked person turns from all their sins, none of their transgressions will be remembered. Past sins are fully erased by genuine repentance.
Do I take any pleasure in the death of the wicked? Rather, am I not pleased when they turn from their ways and live? This reveals the heart of God toward sinners.
But if a righteous person turns from righteousness and commits sin, none of their righteous deeds will be remembered. Past righteousness does not insure against future apostasy.
Repent! Turn away from all offenses. Get a new heart and a new spirit — the imperative form of the promise God will fulfill in 36:26.
Calvin emphasises that God's desire for repentance rather than death is sincere, not merely rhetorical. God's pleasure is in mercy, not in punishment — judgment is his 'strange work' (Isa 28:21).

The command to 'get a new heart' seems paradoxical alongside the promise that God will give a new heart (36:26). Calvin resolves this: the command shows the need; the promise provides the means. Human responsibility and divine sovereignty work together.
The rhetorical question expects the answer 'No' — God emphatically does not desire death. The oracle transforms from legal verdict into passionate appeal.

The people accuse God: 'The way of the LORD is not just.' God reverses the charge: 'Is it my way that is unjust? Is it not your ways that are unjust?' The dispute format reveals the audience's theological rebellion.

Block reads the final appeal as the purpose of the entire chapter: the legal casuistry of vv.1-18 serves the evangelistic appeal of vv.23-32. Individual accountability is not a burden but a liberation — because you are not trapped by your parents' sin, you are free to repent.

Zimmerli sees the dual command ('repent' + 'get a new heart') as reflecting two streams of tradition: the Deuteronomistic call to repentance and the priestly promise of inner transformation. Ezekiel holds both together without resolving the tension — human responsibility and divine grace remain in dialectical relationship.

rows.label: vv.19-24
rows.text: The Wicked Who Repents Lives; the Righteous Who Falls Dies

rows.label: vv.25-32
rows.text: Turn and Live! The Heart of God Revealed

lit.note: The definitive OT statement on individual responsibility and the divine desire for repentance. Legal casuistry serves evangelistic appeal.

themes.note: Justice peaks at 10: individual accountability is the central theme. Mercy at 8: God takes no pleasure in death and pleads for repentance. The chapter balances justice and mercy in rare equilibrium.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: This is Ezekiel's definitive statement on individual responsibility…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Soul Who Sins Shall Die

translations.text: He does not oppress anyone, but returns what he took in pledge for a loan. He does not commit robbery but gives his food to the hungry and provides clothing for the naked.
does not oppress anyone, but restores to the debtor his pledge, commits no robbery, gives his bread to the hungry and covers the naked with a garment,
He does not lend to them at interest or take a profit from them. He withholds his hand from doing wrong and judges fairly between two parties.
does not lend at interest or take any profit, withholds his hand from injustice, executes true justice between man and man,

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Soul Who Sins Shall Die within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
